asserts that the [British](/wiki/British_people "British people") are a [nation](/wiki/Nation "Nation") and promotes the cultural unity of [Britons](/wiki/Britons "Britons"),[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMotyl200162–63-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Guntram_H._Herb_2008-2) in a definition of [Britishness](/wiki/Britishness "Britishness") that may include people of [English](/wiki/English_people "English people"), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people"), [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people"), and [Northern Irish](/wiki/People_of_Northern_Ireland "People of Northern Ireland") descent.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMotyl200162–64-3) British nationalism is closely associated with [British unionism](/wiki/British_unionism "British unionism"), which seeks to uphold the political union that is the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), or strengthen the links between the countries of the United Kingdom.[[4]](#cite_note-Mil133-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Political ideology and movement in the United Kingdom

Not to be confused with [English nationalism](/wiki/English_nationalism "English nationalism").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/ae/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg)

The [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") of the United Kingdom, adopted in this version in 1801 bearing England's red cross with white border (England in 1801 included Wales within it), Ireland's Saint Patrick's Saltire with a white border, and Scotland's Saint Andrew's Saltire and blue background. This is a common symbol used by British nationalists.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Queen_Anne_and_William%2C_Duke_of_Gloucester_by_studio_of_Sir_Godfrey_Kneller.jpg/250px-Queen_Anne_and_William%2C_Duke_of_Gloucester_by_studio_of_Sir_Godfrey_Kneller.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Anne_and_William,_Duke_of_Gloucester_by_studio_of_Sir_Godfrey_Kneller.jpg)

[Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") was the first monarch of the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/MODIS_-_Great_Britain_and_Ireland_-_2012-06-04_during_heat_wave.jpg/250px-MODIS_-_Great_Britain_and_Ireland_-_2012-06-04_during_heat_wave.jpg)](/wiki/File:MODIS_-_Great_Britain_and_Ireland_-_2012-06-04_during_heat_wave.jpg)

Satellite photograph of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"). Originally British nationalism was typically applicable to [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"). British nationalism typically focuses on the unity of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland").

**British nationalism** asserts that the [British](/wiki/British_people "British people") are a [nation](/wiki/Nation "Nation") and promotes the cultural unity of [Britons](/wiki/Britons "Britons"),[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMotyl200162–63-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Guntram_H._Herb_2008-2) in a definition of [Britishness](/wiki/Britishness "Britishness") that may include people of [English](/wiki/English_people "English people"), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people"), [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people"), and [Northern Irish](/wiki/People_of_Northern_Ireland "People of Northern Ireland") descent.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMotyl200162–64-3) British nationalism is closely associated with [British unionism](/wiki/British_unionism "British unionism"), which seeks to uphold the political union that is the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), or strengthen the links between the countries of the United Kingdom.[[4]](#cite_note-Mil133-4)

British nationalism's unifying identity descends from the [ancient Britons](/wiki/Britons_(Celtic_people) "Britons (Celtic people)") who dwelt on the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain").[[2]](#cite_note-Guntram_H._Herb_2008-2) British nationalism grew to include people outside Great Britain, in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), because of the [1542 Crown of Ireland Act](/wiki/Crown_of_Ireland_Act_1542 "Crown of Ireland Act 1542"), which declared that the crown of Ireland was to be held by the ruling monarch of [England](/wiki/England "England") as well as [Anglo-Irish](/wiki/Anglo-Irish "Anglo-Irish") calls for unity with Britain.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

It is characterised as a "powerful but ambivalent force in British politics".[[6]](#cite_note-6) In its moderate form, British nationalism has been a [civic nationalism](/wiki/Civic_nationalism "Civic nationalism"), emphasising both cohesion and diversity of the people of the United Kingdom, its dependencies, and its former colonies.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMotyl200164-7) However, [nativist](/wiki/Nativism_(politics) "Nativism (politics)") nationalism has arisen based on concerns relating to immigration; this [anti-immigrant](/wiki/Opposition_to_immigration "Opposition to immigration") nativist nationalism has manifested politically in the [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party") and other nativist nationalist movements.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMotyl200164-7) Politicians, such as former [British prime minister](/wiki/British_prime_minister "British prime minister") [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron"), have sought to promote British nationalism as a progressive cause.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_nationalism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

During the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), Britain saw the rise of an integrated national economy and a unified [public sphere](/wiki/Public_sphere "Public sphere"), where [British people](/wiki/British_people "British people") began to mobilise on a state-wide scale, rather than just in the smaller units of their province, town, or family.[[9]](#cite_note-9) This period also marked Britain's [emergence](/wiki/Pax_Britannica "Pax Britannica") as the first [global policeman](/wiki/Global_policeman "Global policeman"), with the world's first major [modern navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") and its capital city of [London](/wiki/London "London") establishing itself as the foremost global financial centre.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) The early emergence of a popular patriotic nationalism took place in the mid-18th century and was actively promoted by the British government, as well as writers and intellectuals.[[11]](#cite_note-11) National symbols, anthems, myths, flags and narratives were widely adopted. The [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") was adopted in 1801 as the national flag.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The expansion of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") was accompanied by the awakening of British nationalism. Following the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), segments of British nationalists opposed [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")'s membership of the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community").[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

## Commentary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_nationalism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Commentary")]

Godfried van Benthem van den Bergh in 1966 noted that British nationalism had tended to lack the overt aggression seen in other nationalist movements, instead marked by confidence, a sense of dignity, and some arrogance. He also suggested that the British public rarely identified explicitly as nationalists, favouring patriotism instead.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

James Foley in 2023 wrote that, while philosophical nationalism has often centred the inherent diversity of human cultures, British nationalism in its post-[Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") form focused less on a perceived inherent difference and more on the historic superiority of its liberal traditions and therefore its perceived capacity for international leadership.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In *Britons: Forging the Nation 1707–1837*, [Linda Colley](/wiki/Linda_Colley "Linda Colley") closes by suggesting that the influences that originally bonded Britons are now largely gone, and that these have led to a resurgence of English, Scottish, and Welsh identity.[[14]](#cite_note-Colley-14)

Andrew Black and Luisa Borras in 2021 posited that rising nationalism in the [Devolved Nations](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom") has created tension with British nationalists. Where British nationalism is closely identified with supporting the [monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"), the [houses of parliament](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), the [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciaries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Judiciaries of the United Kingdom") and other elements of the institutional status quo, support for these British institutions is being questioned in the devolved nations.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Nationalism and unionism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_nationalism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Nationalism and unionism")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/British_nationalism "Special:EditPage/British nationalism") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(October 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Nowadays, as in the past, unionist movements exist in [Scotland](/wiki/Unionism_in_Scotland "Unionism in Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Unionism_in_Wales "Unionism in Wales") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland"). Such movements seek specifically to retain the ties between the respective countries and the rest of the [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), in opposition to civic nationalist movements. Such unionist movements include the [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party"), [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") and the [Scottish Unionist Party](/wiki/Scottish_Unionist_Party_(modern) "Scottish Unionist Party (modern)"). In [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), the [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)"), and [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK") parties are in favour of maintaining the Union. British nationalists are generally supportive of unionism. Some argue that modern British nationalism has more right-wing associations tied to it, with it at times being described as [xenophobic](/wiki/Xenophobia "Xenophobia"), whereas [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_nationalism "Scottish nationalism") and [Welsh nationalism](/wiki/Welsh_nationalism "Welsh nationalism") are not viewed in the same way, rather being viewed as progressive[[16]](#cite_note-16).
